Name: jacobian_layer_skipping
Title: Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping for Latency-Aware Inference
Short Hypothesis: A training-free framework that uses Jacobian norms to estimate layer importance and dynamically skips layers based on latency constraints can achieve Pareto-optimal latency-accuracy trade-offs across diverse architectures.
Related Work: Existing works like LiteStage and Similarity-Aware Token Pruning focus on specific architectures or tasks. Our proposal generalizes these ideas by using Jacobian norms to estimate layer importance, enabling architecture-agnostic latency-aware inference. Unlike training-based methods, our approach is entirely training-free, making it more practical for deployment.
Abstract: Latency-aware inference is critical for deploying machine learning models in real-time applications. While existing methods like token pruning and layer skipping offer partial solutions, they often rely on architecture-specific heuristics or training overhead. We propose a training-free framework that leverages Jacobian norms to estimate layer importance and dynamically skips layers based on latency constraints. Our approach enforces strict wall-clock budgets by integrating a latency-aware scheduler, ensuring Pareto-optimal latency-accuracy trade-offs. Experiments on vision transformers, convolutional networks, and language models demonstrate that our framework achieves up to 2.5× speedup with minimal accuracy loss, outperforming existing training-free methods. Theoretical analysis provides guarantees on latency bounds, making our approach both practical and theoretically grounded.
Experiments: Intervention: Implement Jacobian-based layer skipping with a latency-aware scheduler. Skip layers based on Jacobian norms and wall-clock budget.
Evaluation: Compare against baseline models and existing training-free methods (e.g., LiteStage, Similarity-Aware Token Pruning).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Jacobian computation may introduce overhead for very deep models.
Latency-aware scheduler may not generalize to all hardware setups.
Accuracy loss may be significant for models with highly interdependent layers.
